---
title: "DATA3888 Group 6 - Volatility Modelling"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .quarto-figure,
        .quarto-float,
        .cell-output-display {
          max-width: 100%;
          overflow-x: hidden;
        }

        .quarto-figure img,
        img.figure-img {
          max-width: 100%;
          height: auto;
        }
        </style>
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| results: hide

from pathlib import Path
import json

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import display

from back_end.config import FeatureConfig
from back_end.universe import build_model_comparison
from back_end.universe import build_pca_variance_explained

# Change only this path if the raw data directory is somewhere else.
INDIVIDUAL_BOOK_TRAIN_DIR = Path("individual_book_train")


def project_root_from_data_dir(data_dir: Path) -> Path:
    return data_dir.expanduser().resolve().parent


PROJECT_ROOT = project_root_from_data_dir(INDIVIDUAL_BOOK_TRAIN_DIR)


def artifact_path(*parts: str) -> Path:
    return PROJECT_ROOT.joinpath("artifacts", *parts)


RUNS_DIR = artifact_path("runs")
FEATURE_CACHE_PATH = artifact_path("feature_cache", "features_all_stocks.parquet")
FEATURE_CACHE_MANIFEST_PATH = artifact_path("feature_cache", "manifest.json")


def latest_run_dir() -> Path | None:
    if not RUNS_DIR.exists():
        return None
    run_dirs = sorted([path for path in RUNS_DIR.iterdir() if path.is_dir()], reverse=True)
    return run_dirs[0] if run_dirs else None


RUN_DIR = latest_run_dir()


def load_artifact(name: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    if RUN_DIR is None:
        return pd.DataFrame()
    path = RUN_DIR / name
    if not path.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame()
    return pd.read_parquet(path)


def load_feature_targets() -> pd.DataFrame:
    if not FEATURE_CACHE_PATH.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame()
    return pd.read_parquet(
        FEATURE_CACHE_PATH,
        columns=["stock_id", "time_id", "target_var", "target_vol"],
    )


def load_feature_cache() -> pd.DataFrame:
    if not FEATURE_CACHE_PATH.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame()
    return pd.read_parquet(FEATURE_CACHE_PATH)


def load_json(path: Path) -> dict:
    if not path.exists():
        return {}
    return json.loads(path.read_text())


metrics = load_artifact("metrics.parquet")
predictions = load_artifact("predictions.parquet")
feature_importance = load_artifact("feature_importance.parquet")
universe_summary = load_artifact("universe_summary.parquet")
stock_similarity = load_artifact("stock_similarity.parquet")
feature_targets = load_feature_targets()
feature_cache_manifest = load_json(FEATURE_CACHE_MANIFEST_PATH)


def model_summary_frame(metrics_df: pd.DataFrame, predictions_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    if metrics_df.empty or predictions_df.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame()

    metric_cols = ["mse", "rmse", "mae", "mape", "rmspe", "qlike", "pearson_r"]
    available_metrics = [col for col in metric_cols if col in metrics_df.columns]
    overall = metrics_df.copy()
    if "fold" in overall.columns:
        overall = overall[overall["fold"] == 0].copy()

    summary = overall[["model", *available_metrics]].copy()
    latency = (
        predictions_df.groupby("model", as_index=False)
        .agg(
            mean_inference_us=("inference_ms", lambda values: values.mean() * 1000),
            n_predictions=("model", "size"),
        )
    )
    return summary.merge(latency, on="model", how="left")


def cached_model_comparison_table(metrics_df: pd.DataFrame, predictions_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    columns = [
        "Model",
        "Mean inference (μs)",
        "RMSE model wins",
        "↓ QLIKE model wins",
        "RMSE",
        "QLIKE",
        "MAE",
        "MSE",
    ]
    if metrics_df.empty and predictions_df.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

    comparison = build_model_comparison(predictions_df, metrics_df)
    if comparison.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

    table = comparison[
        [
            "model",
            "mean_inference_ms",
            "best_stocks_rmse",
            "best_stocks_qlike",
            "rmse",
            "qlike",
            "mae",
            "mse",
        ]
    ].copy()
    table["mean_inference_ms"] = table["mean_inference_ms"] * 1000
    table = table.rename(
        columns={
            "model": "Model",
            "mean_inference_ms": "Mean inference (μs)",
            "best_stocks_rmse": "RMSE model wins",
            "best_stocks_qlike": "↓ QLIKE model wins",
            "rmse": "RMSE",
            "qlike": "QLIKE",
            "mae": "MAE",
            "mse": "MSE",
        }
    )
    return table.sort_values(
        ["↓ QLIKE model wins", "RMSE", "Model"],
        ascending=[False, True, True],
        na_position="last",
    ).reset_index(drop=True)


def format_report_table(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    formatted = frame.copy()
    integer_cols = ["RMSE model wins", "↓ QLIKE model wins"]
    numeric_cols = [col for col in formatted.columns if col not in ["Model", *integer_cols]]
    for col in integer_cols:
        if col in formatted.columns:
            formatted[col] = formatted[col].map(lambda value: "" if pd.isna(value) else f"{int(value)}")
    for col in numeric_cols:
        formatted[col] = formatted[col].map(lambda value: "" if pd.isna(value) else f"{float(value):.3f}")
    return formatted


def pareto_optimal_mask(frame: pd.DataFrame, x_col: str, y_col: str) -> pd.Series:
    mask = []
    for _, row in frame.iterrows():
        dominated = (
            (frame[x_col] <= row[x_col])
            & (frame[y_col] <= row[y_col])
            & ((frame[x_col] < row[x_col]) | (frame[y_col] < row[y_col]))
        ).any()
        mask.append(not dominated)
    return pd.Series(mask, index=frame.index)

```

# Executive summary (\~1/2 page)

Our project aims to help traders answer the question: "What is the best volatility prediction model for my goal, given latency constraints and given my desired model accuracy?”. To answer this question, we built a front end tool from the ground up, designed to help market participants who need to predict volatility across one or multiple stocks, and need to be able to choose and optimise the best volatility model for the subset of stocks they’re planning to trade. [CITATION HERE: strengthen the motivation with a source on why volatility forecasting matters for trading, market making, risk management, or portfolio choice.] Importantly, we chose to solely focus on the dataset of 112 anonymous stocks due to the higher abundance of data compared to the named stocks. Importantly, the differing lengths of data within a single time id for each dataset prompted us to have to choose a single dataset to work with, to make our analysis uniform and consistent among the two datasets. We note that by default, our tool predicts the mean volatility for the final 30s of data in each time id window, for each stock. Our results show clear separation among different loss functions, and among different stocks. We define a model as winning on a particular stock if that model produces the lowest overall loss for the most time id’s. When choosing the RMSE loss function and evaluating across all stocks in the dataset, we found XGBoost to perform the best by stock wins, with 31 wins and aggregate RMSE of 9.590. Random Forest and LASSO placed second and third by RMSE stock wins, with 17 and 15 wins respectively. When choosing the QLIKE loss function, we found XGBoost to perform the best by stock wins, winning on 55 out of 112 stocks with aggregate QLIKE of 0.496. LASSO and GJR-Garch(1,1) had the second and third most QLIKE wins, with 30 and 22 wins respectively. Importantly however, we note that both Garch models have markedly higher inference latency, with GJR-Garch(1,1) having mean inference latency of 535.147 microseconds compared to XGBoost’s 0.719 microseconds. The difference in inference latency between GJR-Garch(1,1) and XGBoost carries significant execution implications in modern electronic markets. Evidence from Aquilina et al. (2021) establishes that the competitive latency arbitrage races on exchanges possess a modal duration of just 5 to 10 microseconds. While GJR-Garch(1,1) remains useful where latency is less binding, such as portfolio optimisation or risk analysis, the cached results suggest XGBoost offers the strongest balance of stock level accuracy and speed in this experiment. We find these core findings to be highly relevant for market makers and quantitative researchers who must regularly make the choice between minimising latency and having the models that fit the data best. As shown above, we see clear tradeoffs between model inference latency and model loss. Our tool exposes this to traders, and lets them make informed decisions that most fit their specific strategy and the universe of assets they wish to trade.



```{python}

```

# Background

Our research question is: what is the most accurate volatility prediction model for a given universe of stocks, given latency constraints?

Traders use volatility forecasting models for many reasons, including but not limited to modelling inventory risk, portfolio optimisation and value at risk VaR analysis. Depending on the application, traders, market makers and portfolio managers require tools which have different accuracy vs latency tradeoffs. [CITATION HERE: cite market microstructure or algorithmic trading work showing latency can be economically meaningful.] For instance, a portfolio manager calculating their 1 day VaR may want the most accurate model (particularly in the tails of a given returns distribution), while a researcher creating an options pricing algorithm for a highly liquid stock may prioritise speed at the cost of some accuracy. Hence, it is highly important for market participants to choose the model that is the best fit for their given problem.

Tools like Tradingview let traders view the price over time, and introduce scripting tools which help traders visualise different functions of price, eg. rolling volatility over time (TradingView, n.d.). However, they do not introduce any way for traders to predict future volatility. In order to do this, traders currently must spend significant time to program a volatility prediction model of their choosing, create visuals for that model etc. Many traders simply want to know the accuracy of a given volatility prediction model, compared to the inference time that that model takes. Our tool innovates on the current tooling landscape by providing a no code interface, which lets traders and market participants view model latency vs model accuracy tradeoffs in a simple, intuitive manner.

We were given two datasets to work with: a dataset of 112 anonymous, no name stocks, and a dataset of 10 named stocks. Each dataset contained the top two levels of orderbook data, where prices series were split up into differing time ids. [CITATION HERE: cite the dataset documentation/course handout for stock counts, order-book levels, time ids, and anonymity.] As the length of the price series within each time id were not constant between the two datasets, we ultimately chose to model the dataset of 112 anonymous stocks, due to it’s higher abundance of data, and to ensure that modelling and data constraints were consistent between stocks. This gave us a wider cross section of assets while keeping the modelling target and preprocessing assumptions consistent across the full experiment. Notably, each time id contains 600 seconds of data and the temporal ordering of each time id has not been preserved.

We introduce two different classes of models: time series models, and parametric models. The parametric models we analysed include XGBoost, multivariate linear regression, lasso, ridge regression, random forest and decision trees. The time series models we analysed include regular GARCH(1,1) and GJR-Garch(1,1). [CITATION HERE: cite the original or standard references for GARCH and GJR-GARCH.]

# Methods

@fig-workflow summarises how data moves through preprocessing, model configuration, prediction, evaluation, and to the dashboard.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-workflow
%%| fig-cap: "End to end volatility modelling workflow. Order-book data is transformed into modelling features, passed through user selected model specifications, evaluated against the final 30 second realised volatility target, and displayed in the dashboard."
flowchart TD
    A["Raw order-book data"] --> B["Preprocess prices and returns"]
    B --> C["Load or compute feature cache"]
    C --> D["Select models and feature mode"]

    D --> E{"Model class"}
    E -- "Parametric / ML" --> F["Cross validated training"]
    E -- "GARCH family" --> G["Windo level volatility forecasts"]

    F --> H["Predict final 30 second volatility"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Evaluate accuracy and inference latency"]
    I --> J["Save run artifacts"]
    J --> K["Dashboard views"]
```

```{python}

```

We used two separate approaches for each model class. For parametric models, we randomly assign each stock to one of five folds of data, calculate features based on the first 9 minutes and 30 seconds of data, and train each model using the 4 training folds. [CITATION HERE: cite cross-validation methodology, or explain why stock-level folds are appropriate for testing cross-stock generalisation.] We then use those trained models to predict the mean variance of the final 30 seconds of data for each time id, for each stock in the single remaining out of sample fold. We then repeat this process for every stock in the dataset, such that each individual stock has one volatility prediction per model per time id, where the model was not trained on any data from that particular stock. This process effectively means we are training a single model across multiple different time ids. For our time series models, as we do not know the temporal ordering of the time ids, we trained a new model on the first 9.5 minutes of data for each time id for each stock. In inference, we then unconditionally walk each Garch model forward, predicting the point variance for each second of the final 30 seconds of data, then use those point estimates to calculate the mean variance for the final 30 seconds of each time id.


traders can select either a subset of features to use, or the top n principal components of those features.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-cumulative-variance
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative variance explained by the first 20 principal components calculated from the cached modelling features."

pca_variance = build_pca_variance_explained(load_feature_cache(), FeatureConfig(), 20)

if pca_variance.empty:
    print("No cached feature data was available for the PCA cumulative variance figure.")
else:
    plot_df = pca_variance.copy()
    plot_df["cumulative_variance_ratio"] = plot_df["explained_variance_ratio"].cumsum()

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.8))
    ax.plot(
        plot_df["component"],
        plot_df["cumulative_variance_ratio"],
        color="#f59f00",
        linewidth=2.5,
        marker="o",
        markersize=5,
    )
    ax.set_title("Cumulative Variance Explained", fontweight="bold", loc="left")
    ax.set_xlabel("Principal component")
    ax.set_ylabel("Cumulative variance")
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
    ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(lambda value, _: f"{value:.0%}")
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25)
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

```

When a time series in a particular time id was missing data, we forward filled past values such that we have a continuous series of values without any future leakage. If the time id was missing too much data, having \< 20 price updates across the first 570 seconds, or having \< 5 price updates in the final 30 seconds of data, we dropped that particular time id from our calculations, as to not attempt to get models to predict volatility with an inadequate amount of information.

Moreover, we faced some challenges with exploding volatility predictions from unstable Garch fits. To mitigate this, we treated Garch forecasts as missing when the fitted parameters failed the stationarity checks, when boundary parameters were at or above 0.99, or when the forecast variance path was non-finite, negative, or unreasonably large. [CITATION HERE: cite GARCH stationarity/positivity constraints, and consider adding the exact stationarity condition used in the code.] In a similar fashion, the linear regression models would occasionally predict negative volatility, which was causing significant distortions when measuring QLIKE loss. To prevent negative volatility predictions, we clipped all predictions of negative volatility to the first percentile of training volatility for that fold. Note, this only influenced 3 out of 428,960 time ids across the data, and made the results between models directly comparable. Prior to this change, the volatility predictions for these 3 time ids accounted for approximately \~86% of total QLIKE loss, creating meaningful distortion separate from genuine model accuracy. Our changes helped prevent this distortion.

Hence, the workflow of our product is: If a results cache exists, traders can view the results from that cache without having to recompute results. Otherwise, if they wish to re-calculate their results or specify different models or model parameters, they can move onto the next step Features are pulled from the feature, or computed as soon as the front end is opened if no cache exists Principal components are calculated from those features Traders specify model(s) they want to use Traders then press run, where the platform will predict the the mean volatility for the final 30s of data for each time id for each stock Traders can then observe their data in the individual stock, and stock universe pages

Notably, we chose to include MSE, RMSE, MAE, and QLIKE loss functions in our model evaluation. QLIKE loss is both the industry standard loss metric for volatility prediction, and the main metric we used in our results, as it asymmetrically punishes under predicting volatility. [CITATION HERE: cite volatility forecast evaluation literature for QLIKE, especially its use with noisy realised volatility proxies.] This is especially important in financial applications, because underestimating market risk can lead to more serious consequences than overestimating it.

SHOULD MENTION WHAT THE HAR-RV MODEL IS EXACTLY.

```{python}

```

# Results

‘Best’ model is subjective. Depends highly on the given loss function you want to optimise and your goal

The cached model comparison table summarises each model's average inference latency, stock level wins under RMSE and QLIKE, and aggregate error metrics.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-model-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "Cached model comparison across all stocks. Lower error metrics are better; model wins count the number of stocks where the model had the lowest loss for that metric."

model_comparison = cached_model_comparison_table(metrics, predictions)
if model_comparison.empty:
    print("No cached model comparison results were available.")
else:
    display(format_report_table(model_comparison))

```

We see the model with both the lowest QLIKE loss on average, and the most wins on all stocks is XGBoost, with 55 wins and the lowest mean QLIKE loss of 0.496. This is followed closely by lasso with 30 wins and mean QLIKE loss of 0.518, and trailed farther behind by GJR-Garch(1,1) with 22 wins, though worse tail performance in QLIKE loss increasing it's mean QLIKE loss to 0.596. No other models win on more than 2 stocks, though we note consistent performance from the Random Forest, decision tree, and linear regression models.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pareto-frontier
#| fig-cap: "Pareto frontier of model QLIKE against cached mean inference latency. Lower values are better on both axes."

model_summary = model_summary_frame(metrics, predictions)
pareto_frame = model_summary.dropna(subset=["model", "mean_inference_us", "qlike"]).copy()
pareto_frame = pareto_frame[pareto_frame["mean_inference_us"] > 0].copy()

if pareto_frame.empty:
    print("No cached model metrics were available for the Pareto frontier.")
else:
    optimal = pareto_optimal_mask(pareto_frame, "mean_inference_us", "qlike")
    q1, q3 = pareto_frame["qlike"].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
    iqr = q3 - q1
    qlike_cap = max(q3 + 1.5 * iqr, pareto_frame["qlike"].median())
    qlike_cap = min(qlike_cap, pareto_frame["qlike"].max())
    pareto_frame["qlike_plot"] = pareto_frame["qlike"].clip(upper=qlike_cap)
    pareto_frame["qlike_clipped"] = pareto_frame["qlike"] > qlike_cap
    pareto_models = pareto_frame[optimal].sort_values("mean_inference_us")
    dominated_models = pareto_frame[~optimal].sort_values("mean_inference_us")

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

    if not dominated_models.empty:
        ax.scatter(
            dominated_models["mean_inference_us"],
            dominated_models["qlike_plot"],
            color="gray",
            s=50,
            alpha=0.7,
            label="Dominated models",
            zorder=3,
        )

    ax.plot(
        pareto_models["mean_inference_us"],
        pareto_models["qlike_plot"],
        color="red",
        linewidth=1.5,
        label="Pareto frontier",
        zorder=2,
    )
    ax.scatter(
        pareto_models["mean_inference_us"],
        pareto_models["qlike_plot"],
        color="red",
        s=60,
        zorder=4,
        label="Pareto optimal models",
    )

    label_offsets = {
        "LASSO": (-5, 9),
        "Ridge Regression": (5, 9),
        "Decision Tree": (5, -3),
        "Linear Regression": (7, 6),
        "Random Forest": (7, -11),
        "GARCH(1,1)": (6, -10),
        "GJR-GARCH(1,1)": (6, -10),
    }
    label_alignments = {"LASSO": "right"}

    for _, row in pareto_frame.sort_values("mean_inference_us").iterrows():
        is_optimal = row["model"] in set(pareto_models["model"])
        label = row["model"]
        if row["qlike_clipped"]:
            label = f"{label} ({row['qlike']:.2f})"
        xytext = label_offsets.get(row["model"], (5, 5 if is_optimal else -10))
        ax.annotate(
            label,
            xy=(row["mean_inference_us"], row["qlike_plot"]),
            xytext=xytext,
            textcoords="offset points",
            fontsize=8,
            color="red" if is_optimal else "gray",
            ha=label_alignments.get(row["model"], "left"),
        )

    for x, color, label in [
        (1, "green", "1 microsecond"),
        (10, "gold", "10 microseconds"),
        (1000, "purple", "1000 microseconds"),
    ]:
        ax.axvline(x=x, color=color, linestyle="--", linewidth=1.2, label=label)

    ax.set_xscale("log")
    ax.set_xlabel("Inference time (microseconds)")
    ax.set_ylabel("QLIKE (lower is better)")
    ax.set_title("Pareto Frontier: Volatility Model Selection")
    ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
    ax.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":", alpha=0.4)
    ax.set_xlim(
        max(pareto_frame["mean_inference_us"].min() * 0.6, 0.01),
        pareto_frame["mean_inference_us"].max() * 2,
    )
    ax.set_ylim(
        max(pareto_frame["qlike_plot"].min() - 0.08, 0),
        pareto_frame["qlike_plot"].max() + 0.15,
    )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

```


We see the pareto frontier of model accuracy (measured in QLIKE) plotted against inference time (measured in microseconds). We see the XGBoost model dominates in terms of overall loss, though has marginally higher inference time of 719 nanoseconds compared to lasso's 227 nanoseconds. Hence, for ultra high frequency applications, traders may prefer the lower latency of lasso, while traders optimising for model accuracy may prefer XGBoost. Notably, we see the significant gap between the inference time of the Garch models and the parametric models. Garch models have mean inference latency on the order of 500 microseconds while parametric models are up to 4 orders of magnitude faster in the case of the HAR-RV model. [CITATION HERE: check this mentions HAR-RV even though HAR-RV is not listed in the model set above; either cite/introduce HAR-RV or change this to the correct model.] This makes contextual sense considering the nature of unconditional volatility forecasting, where Garch models must in this case, run inference on for 30 different points separately and then average each of those predictions, as opposed to the parametric models which can predict the mean volatility of the final 30 seconds of data with a single forward pass.

# Discussion

RE-WRITE THIS WHOLE DISCUSSION SECTION. IT'S A CHATGPT GENERATED SUGGESTION. 


# Conclusion



# Student contributions

Eriks wrote most of the front end, back end, and first draft of the report….. Continue this.

# References (use apa 7)

Aquilina, M.,Budish, E., & O’Neill, P. (2021). Quantifying the high frequency trading “arms race” (BIS Working Paper No. 955). Bank for International Settlements. https://www.bis.org/publ/work955.pdf

TradingView. (n.d.). Visuals overview. Pine Script documentation. https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/visuals/overview/
